W

description: The (somewhat simpler) Mahasanghika parallel to [the Satipaṭṭhāna Sutta](/content/canon/mn10).

title: Iti 1 Lobha Sutta: Greed
description:
> Greed is that one thing, bhikkhus.
Abandon that and I guarantee you non-return.

Abandoning greed is most, though not all, of the path.

title: Iti 100 Brāhmaṇa Dhamma Yāga Sutta: The Holy Offering of the Teaching
description:
> You are my children, my sons, born from my mouth, born of the Dhamma...

The Buddha compares himself and his disciples to the Brahmins, and encourages his community to be as open-handed with the Dha

title: Iti 105 Taṇhuppāda Sutta: The Arising of Craving
description:
> With craving his companion, a man  
wanders on a long, long time.

What can cause a monk to be reborn?

title: Iti 107 Bahukāra Sutta: Very Helpful
description:
> Householders and homeless alike,  
Each a support for the other

The reciprocal ways in which monks and lay supporters benefit each other.

title: Iti 109 Nadīsota Sutta: A River
description:
> Suppose, bhikkhus, a man was being borne along by the current of a river...

An extended metaphor for the dangers of “going with the flow.”

title: Iti 111 Sampanna Sīla Sutta: Perfect in Virtue
description:
> A bhikkhu who in such a manner is ardent and afraid of wrongdoing is called constantly energetic and resolute.

A short sutta stressing the foundation of ethics on which insight meditation must rest

title: Iti 17 Dutiyasekha Sutta: A Trainee (2)
description:
> I do not perceive another single factor so helpful as good friendship...

Friendship with admirable people is the prime external factor to help those in training.

title: Iti 19 Saṁgha Sāmaggī Sutta: Harmony in the Saṅgha
authors: geoff # this translation strongly prefered here
description:
> Blissful is concord in the Saṅgha.

Concord in the Sangha leads to the welfare and happiness of many beings, both human and divine.

title: Iti 20 Paduṭṭhacitta Sutta: A Corrupted Mind
description:
> Beings go to a bad bourn  
Because of mind’s corruption.

<!---->

title: Iti 21 Pasanna Citta Sutta: A Confident Mind
description:
> ... if this person were to die at this time, as if carried there he would be placed in heaven.

<!---->

title: Iti 22 Metta Sutta: The Benefits of Love
description:
> Mendicants, don’t fear good deeds. For ‘good deeds’ is a term for happiness...

The Buddha recalls the results he himself has experienced from doing meritorious deeds.

title: Iti 24 Aṭṭhipuñja Sutta: A Heap of Bones
description:
> The accumulation  
of a single person’s  
bones for an eon  
would be a heap  
on a par with a mountain

> … of all the grounds for making worldly merit, none are worth a sixteenth part of the heart’s release by love.

Goodwill far outshines all other ways of making merit.

Three types of people: one like a cloud without rain, one who rains locally, and one who rains everywhere.

> By not halting, friend, and by not straining I crossed the flood.

How the Buddha crossed the flood of suffering.

description:
> They do not know bliss  
Who have not seen Nandana

A deva thinks his pleasures are supreme.

title: SN 1.12 Nandati Sutta: Delight
description:
> Standing to one side, that deity recited this verse...

We think our attachments bring us happiness, but they really bring sorrow.

title: SN 1.20 Samiddhi Sutta: Samiddhi
description:
> … sensual pleasures are time-consuming, full of suffering and despair, and the danger in them is greater still

A deva tried to convince a young monk to enjoy sensual pleasures and the Buddha rebuke

title: SN 1.23 Jaṭā Sutta: The Tangle
description:
> Where name-and-form ceases,  
Stops without remainder,  
And also impingement and perception of form:  
It is here this tangle is cut.

This generation is all tangled up like a nest of matted hair.

title: SN 1.24 Manonivāraṇa Sutta: Reining in the Mind
description:
> Should one rein in the mind from everything...

Or only from what is unwholesome?

title: SN 1.33 Sādhu Sutta: Good
description: A series of deities give verses praising generosity and the Buddha gives his own response.

title: SN 1.36 Saddhā Sutta: Faith
description:
> No ties torment one who has nothing

A series of Satullapa gods address the Buddha in verse.

description:
> One should not pursue a course  
That is painful and harmful.

Kokanada gives a pithy teaching in verse.

title: SN 1.41 Āditta Sutta: Ablaze
description:
> When one’s house is ablaze  
The vessel taken out  
Is the one that is useful,  
Not the one left burnt inside.

A deity recites some verses to the Buddha.

title: SN 1.42 Kiṁdada Sutta: Giving What
description:
> Giving food you give strength.  
Giving clothes you give beauty.

A few verses on the results of giving particularly praising the giving of shelter.

title: SN 1.46 Accharā Sutta: Nymphs
description:
> ‘The straight way’ that path is called,  
And ‘fearless’ is its destination.

To escape from the Forest of Delusion, one needs the vehicle of the Dhamma.

title: SN 1.49 Macchari Sutta: Samiddhi

> These brighten up the heavens  
Where they’ve been reborn.

The Buddha is asked about the future destiny of people who are generous—and not.

The world is burning with desire.

> What is the one thing, O Gotama,
Whose killing you approve?

> What here is a man’s best treasure?

A series of questions on what is best.

> What should one who desires the good

> This divine vehicle unsurpassed

Inevitably winning the victory.

description:
> And how does a bhikkhu who has a good friend develop and cultivate the Noble Eightfold Path?

description:

title: SN 45.8 Vibhaṅga Sutta: Analysis
description:
The Canonical definition of the Noble Eightfold Path.

For an even more detailed analysis, see [MN 117](/content/canon/mn117).

title: SN 45.9 Sūka Sutta: A Spike
description:
> … a bhikkhu develops right view, which is based upon seclusion, dispassion, and cessation, maturing in release

On the kind of right view necessary to attain Nibbāna.

description: Just as living creatures are based on the earth, the awakening factors are based on ethics.

title: SN 46.16 Tatiya Gilāna Sutta: The Third Discourse on Illness
description: When the Buddha was sick, Mahācunda recited for him the awakening factors.

title: SN 46.2 Kāya Sutta: The Body

Just as the body depends on food, the hindrances and the awak

description: A sutta on how *samādhi* is squandered by unwise attention.

title: SN 46.55 Saṅgārava Sutta: Saṅgarava

title: SN 47.10 Bhikkhunupassaya Sutta: The Nuns’ Quarters

title: SN 47.13 Cunda Sutta: Cunda
description:
> … dwell with yourselves as your own island, with yourselves as your own refuge

title: SN 47.15 Bāhiya Sutta: With Bāhiya
description: When Venerable Bāhiya asks for a teaching to take on retreat, the Buddha teaches the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, well grounded on ethics.

title: SN 47.19 Sedaka Sutta: The Acrobat Simile
description: > Protecting oneself, bhikkhus, one protects others; protecting others, one protects oneself. For an audio recording of this sutta, see [this entry](/content/canon/sn47.19_candasiri).

title: SN 47.19 Sedaka Sutta: The Acrobat Simile (recording)
description:
> Protecting oneself, bhikkhus, one protects others; protecting others, one protects oneself.

A reading of [SN 47.19](/content/canon/sn47.19).

title: SN 47.3 Bhikkhu Sutta: A Bhikkhu
description: The Buddha summarizes the path as the four foundations of mindfulness based on virtue.

title: SN 47.31 Ananussuta Sutta: Unheard Before

title: SN 47.4 Sālā Sutta: At Sālā

Compassionate, knows the one-way path

Brahma Sahampati praises the Buddha's reflections on the power of Satipaṭṭhāna Meditation.

title: SN 47.48 Mitta Sutta: Friends
description: You should encourage your friends to practice the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

title: SN 47.6 Sakuṇagghi Sutta: The Hawk
description:
> Move in your own resort, bhikkhus, in your own ancestral domain. Mara will not gain access to those who move in their own resort.

The parable of the quail and the hawk.

title: SN 47.7 Makkaṭa Sutta: A Monkey

title: SN 47.8 Sūda Sutta: The Cook
description:
> ... that wise, competent, skilful bhikkhu picks up the sign of his own mind.

Like a cook, a meditator must attend to the signs of their success.

title: SN 47.9 Gilāna Sutta: Sick

A sutta good to contemplate or chant, the analysis of the five spiritua

title: SN 48.17 Tatiya Vitthāra Sutta: The Third in Detail

description: One who understands the origin, the passing, the gratification, the danger, and the escape regarding the five faculties is a stream-enterer.

title: SN 48.40 The Uppaṭipāṭika Sutta: Irregular Order
description: A fascinating description of the four jhānas and nirodha as the cessation of pain, sadness, pleasure, happiness, and equanimity respectively.

title: SN 48.41 Jarādhamma Sutta: Old Age
description:
> … the limbs are flabby & wrinkled; the back, bent forward

When Ānanda sees the Buddha’s sense faculties fading, the Buddha speaks on the decrepitude of old age.

description:

Having developed wisdom a mendicant is awakened.

title: SN 48.50 Āpaṇa Sutta: At Āpaṇa on Faith
description: Venerable Sāriputta explains how a faithful disciple uses their faith to develop the path.

title: SN 48.54 Pada Sutta: Footprints
description: Just as all footprints fit into that of an elephant, wisdom is the chief of qualities that lead to awakening.

title: SN 48.8 Daṭṭhabbaṁ Sutta: To Be Seen
description:
> The faculty of conviction, the faculty of persistence, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, & the faculty of discernment.

The Buddha defines the Five Spiritual Faculties (*indr

title: SN 48.9 Paṭhama Vibhaṅga Sutta: The First Analysis
description: The Buddha defines the five spiritual faculties.

> One to whom it might occur,  
‘I'm a woman’ or ‘I'm a man’  
Or ‘I'm anything at all’--  
Is fit for Māra to address.

Sāriputta asks why Anuruddha looks so bright, and he replies that it

title: SN 54.11 Icchānaṅgala Sutta: At Icchānaṅgala

title: SN 55.1 Cakkavatti Rāja Sutta: A Wheel-Turning Monarch

title: SN 55.11 Sahassa Bhikkhunisaṁgha Sutta: A Thousand Nuns

One of the few suttas in the Canon where the Buddha directly teaches Bhikkhunīs.

You should encourage your friends in the Dhamma.

description:
> "It’s incredible, it’s amazing! Who can’t become a stream-enterer these days?"

It's never too late to practice.

title: SN 55.38 Vassa Sutta: Rain
description:
> In the same way, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha...

Like rain falling on the mountain top, the four factors of stream-entry flow on to the ending of defilements.

description:

description:
> When a noble disciple has these four streams of merit [...] his merit simply is incalculable

The four factors of stream-entry—with wisdom as the fourth—are called streams of merit.

title: SN 55.44 Paṭhamamahaddhana Sutta: Rich (1st)
description:
> … a noble disciple who has four things is said to be rich, prosperous, and wealthy.

The four factors of stream-entry are said to be true prosperity.

title: SN 55.5 The Sāriputta Sutta: To Sāriputta

> Association with people of integrity, lord, is a factor for stream entry.

description:
> who has confidence in the Saṅgha,  
and correct view:  
they’re said to be prosperous,  
their life is not in vain.

The four factors of stream entry.

title: SN 55.52 Vassaṁvuttha Sutta: One Who Completed the Rains

> … how a wise lay follower should advise another wise lay follower who is sick

Ending with a rather unusual description of the path as turning the mind progressively higher.

description:
> Bhikkhus, if one’s clothes or head were ablaze, what should be done about it?

Even more than if your clothes are on fire, you should make an effort to understand the four noble truths.

title: SN 56.35 Sattisata Sutta: A Hundred Spears
description:
> Rather, the breakthrough to the Four Noble Truths is accompanied only by happiness and joy.

Even more than if you’re being tortured with spikes, you should make an effort to realize Nibbāna.

title: SN 56.36 Pāṇa Sutta: Living Creatures

[...] So vast, bhikkhus,

description:
> Therefore, bhikkhus, an exertion should be made to understand...

As the dawn precedes the sunrise, right view precedes the penetration of the four noble truths.

title: SN 56.38 Dutiyasūriya Sutta: The Second Simile of the Sun

No-one can refute you if you are well grounded in

title: SN 56.48 Dutiyachiggaḷayuga Sutta: A Yoke With a Hole (2)
description: In this famous simile, the Buddha explains how rare it is to receive a human rebirth in the time of a Buddha and encourages us to use the opportunity well.

The Buddha explains the fruit of Stream Entry.

description:
> Bhikkhus, do not engage in disputatious talk

Don’t argue. Instead, converse on the four noble truths.

What a Buddha bows to.

Brahmā Tudu tries to persuade Kokālika to have faith in Sāriputta and Moggallāna.

> Again & again one wearies & trembles.

A brahmin complains when the Buddha visits for alms many days in a row.

The Buddha gives encouragement to a brahmin seeking alms for his parents.

As we advance along the path, we shed our old attachments.

title: Snp 1.3 Khaggavisāṇa Sutta: The Rhinceros Horn Sutta

If you can’t find a good teacher, it’s better to wander alone than to consort with fools.

title: Snp 1.4 Kasi Bharadvaja Sutta:: To the Plowing Bharadvaja
description:

Sometimes people will reference suttas by their Pāli name. This handy index helps you to map that Pāli name (e.g. "Madhurā Sutta") to its Nikāya and number (e.g. "MN 84").

title: Dictionary of Pāli Proper Names
description:
This dictionary contains thousands of entries covering the people, places, animals, books, etc. of the Pāli Canon.

You can hear the pronunciation of some of these names [over at ReadingFaithfully.org

title: Pāḷi Numbers (Saṅkhyā)
description: A large table of numbers in Pāli, including some fractional numbers found in Pāli literature.

title: Pāli Pronunciation Recordings
description: A series of recordings demonstrating how to pronounce some Pāli names and terms you may come across while reading the suttas.

title: Pāli Script Converter
description: The Pāli Language has been written in many different scripts over the years: Thai, Lao, and even Roman. This helpful tool will automatically convert Pāli (or Sanskrit) text between any two scripts.

title: Pāli-Thai-English-Sanskrit Dictionary
description:

A bilingual English/Thai Dictionary of Pāli with Sanskrit equivalents listed for each entry.

title: Pāli Keybindings for Windows
description: Two keyboard shortcut programs for Windows making "Pāḷi" easier to type.

title: The PTS Pali-English Dictionary
description:

title: Photo Dharma
description: > Over 15,000 photographs of Buddhist archeological sites, pilgrimage centres, and temples in SE Asia, as well as Videos, Maps, Posters, etc.

title: Pleco
description:
> The world's best Chinese dictionaries

description:

title: Pressing Out Pure Honey
description: A short summary of every *sutta* in the Majjhima Nikāya.

title: Reading Faithfully
description:
If you are looking to dive into the suttas, there are many good pointers and valuable resources to be found on this blog.

A good place to start, is their [How To Guide](https://www.readingfaithfully.

title: Realms of Rebirth Taught by the Buddha
description: Reference charts that map out the realms of rebirth as understood by the Theravāda Tradition.

description:
A spreadsheet showing the reconstructed "proper" order of the SA discourses, along with a table of their known parallels in the Pāli and Āgama Canons.

description:

title: Stages of Enlightenment
description: A small chart summarizing the four stages of enlightenment.

title: SuttaCentral Voice
description: SuttaCentral Voice allows you to search and listen to the Suttas in Pāli and English.

title: SuttaCentral
description: SuttaCentral hosts sources and free translations of Early Buddhist Texts, meticulously organized by parallels, books, and languages and searchable with several large indexes and built-in dictionary to

description: A beautiful tree graphing out the full organizational structure of the Sutta Piṭaka.

title: Table of Solutions [to Global Warming]
description: A fairly comprehensive overview of the many, many things we can do now to reduce society's carbon footprint along with estimates of each project's cost and effectiveness.

title: Thai-English Buddhist Dictionary
description: A collection of Thai Buddhist terms (mostly taken directly from the Pāli) along with their English glosses.

description: A digital, guided meditation exploring the parts of the body.

title: The Tibetan and Himalayan Library
description: A large collection of short writings and media on a variety of Tibetan cultural topics.

title: Ti-Civara (Website)
description: A web-app for helping do the calculations necessary for Thai-style robe sewing, along with other helpful information about dyeing, tag knots, borders, and more.

title: Tipiṭaka.org
description:
The full Pāli Canon (along with its commentaries) searchable online in a variety of scripts.

I personally prefer [the DPR](/content/reference/dpr).

title: Train Chinese
description: An excellent collection of apps for phones, tablets and desktop computers for learning the fundamentals of Chinese. I especially recommend their mobile app "Pinyin Trainer" for learning the phonetics

title: Vandana Book: Homage to the Buddha, Dhamma and Sangha in Pali, Sinhala, and English
description: A trilingual compilation of devotional hymns, vows, and meditations for chanting from the Sri Lankan tradition.

title: Virtual Angkor
description: A series of handcrafted 3D animations and explanatory essays giving a feel for what Angkor would have looked like back in its prime in the 13th century CE.
